title: Star Wars: The New Jedi Order
content:
{{Top|rwm|njo|italics=1}}
{{Otheruses|New Jedi Order}}
{{Update|{{YouTube|video=gpyIPVRM5Ak|text=Legacy Expo 2025 - Saga for the Ages: New Jedi Order|channel=@LegacyExpo|name=Legacy Expo|date=2025-10-06}}}}

{{Quote|I have fought the worst of all wars, and witnessed the redemption of evil. I've seen balance restored to the Force. But order can turn to chaos… as it did when I was born. Now, with my loved ones and my loyal allies, I face a new challenge unlike any before. And I'm not sure if this time we can win.|Luke Skywalker|<ref name="Commercial">{{SWArchive|url=eu/lit/novel/1.html|text=''Vector Prime'' Television Commercial}}</ref>}}
'''''Star Wars: The New Jedi Order''''', commonly abbreviated as the '''''NJO''''', is a multi-author book series that was published by [[Del Rey]] between [[1999]] and [[2003]], consisting of nineteen novels, three [[eBook]] novellas, and three short stories. Twelve authors in total contributed to the series, which was a collaborative effort conceived by representatives of Del Rey, [[Lucasfilm]], and [[Dark Horse Comics]] during meetings at [[Skywalker Ranch]] in [[1997]] and [[1998]]. The NJO is a chronological storyline covering a four-[[Standard year/Legends|year]] span of [[The galaxy/Legends|galactic]] history, beginning [[25 ABY/Legends|twenty-one years after]] the events of [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']] and telling the story of an [[Yuuzhan Vong War|invasion]] of the galaxy by a race of [[Extragalactic/Legends|extra-galactic]] religious zealots.

Within the NJO's pages, the [[Yuuzhan Vong]] begin a massive invasion of the galaxy, and quickly gain ground despite efforts at repulsion made by the [[New Republic/Legends|New Republic]] and [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker's]] [[New Jedi Order]]. Skywalker, [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]], [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia Organa Solo]], and a new generation of heroes battle the [[Blood/Legends|bloodthirsty]] fanatics as they overtake an unprepared Republic, [[Death/Legends|killing]] untold numbers of innocents and devastating countless [[Planet/Legends|planets]], even [[Battle of Coruscant (Yuuzhan Vong War)|conquering]] the galactic [[Capital/Legends|capital]] of [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]]. Eventually, a rebuilt [[Galactic Federation of Free Alliances|galactic government]], aided by the [[Jedi/Legends|Jedi]] and the mysterious living planet [[Zonama Sekot/Legends|Zonama Sekot]], overthrow the Yuuzhan Vong and save the galaxy. Characters from the [[Original trilogy|original ''Star Wars'' trilogy]] of films play large roles, as do established [[Star Wars Legends|Expanded Universe]] characters and new additions to the galaxy far, far away, all working together to repel the invaders.

As a large-scale project the likes of which ''[[Star Wars]]'' had never seen before, the NJO was an undertaking that was supervised closely by Lucasfilm and kept consistent by a team of [[Canon|continuity]] advisors. Every book in the series made it onto the [[Wikipedia: New York Times Best Seller List|New York Times Best Seller List]], and paved the way for future multi-author projects such as [[Star Wars: Legacy of the Force|''Legacy of the Force'']] and [[Star Wars: Fate of the Jedi|''Fate of the Jedi'']]. Several tie-in sourcebooks, magazine articles, and [[Star Wars comic books|comic books]] were published, helping to expand the [[New Jedi Order era]] within the ''Star Wars'' mythos.

==Development==
===Conception===
{{Quote|It goes against all the safe conditions of putting together a series of books. Safe would have said copy what's happened before. Safe would have said don't do anything bigger than a trilogy. They chose a more gutsy approach. I salute them for it.|R.A. Salvatore|<ref name="Salvatore interview page 2">{{WebCite|url=http://theforce.net/jedicouncil/interview/salvatore2.shtml|text=Cellblock 1138: R.A. Salvatore - Part 2|work=[[TheForce.net]]|archivedate=20190730151801}}</ref>}}
[[Bantam Spectra]] and [[Lucasfilm]] had been publishing ''[[Star Wars]]'' novels written by various authors throughout the 1990s, beginning with [[1991]]'s ''[[Heir to the Empire]]'', which quickly reached #1 on the [[Wikipedia:New York Times Best Seller list|New York Times Best Seller list]]. Following the book's success, both companies agreed that every subsequent novel would form a part of a larger cohesive [[Canon|continuity]].<ref name="World SW Novels">''The World of STAR WARS Novels'' (''[[X-Wing: Rogue Squadron]]'' paperback)</ref> In [[1997]], the publishing contract for ''Star Wars'' novels was renegotiated with [[Del Rey]], the publishing imprint of [[Ballantine Books]], and the decision was made to produce a large series which would take several years to publish.<ref name="Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview">[[Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview|''Star Wars: New Jedi Order'' Round-Robin Interview]]</ref><ref name="Novel Approach">{{InsiderCite|66|Novel Approach}}</ref> Under the Bantam license, the majority of novels released had been trilogies or stand-alones; despite the continuity, many novels were being written simultaneously and published in close succession. This often led to stories which took place later in the timeline being published prior to stories which took place earlier.<ref name="The Corellian Trilogy">''[[Star Wars: The Corellian Trilogy]]''</ref><ref name="The Black Fleet Crisis">''[[The Black Fleet Crisis]]''</ref><ref name="The New Rebellion">''[[The New Rebellion]]''</ref> The planned Del Rey series, however, would be one large chronological story line covering a five-[[Standard year/Legends|year]] span of [[The galaxy/Legends|galactic]] history.<ref name="unjoh interviews: Liang">{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/books/unjoh/interviews/index.html#liang|text=Kuo-Yu Liang Interview|work=[[TheForce.net|The Unofficial NJO Homepage]]|archivedate=20190730225123}}</ref>

[[File:Shapiro.jpg|thumb|left|Shelly Shapiro, one of the chief architects of ''The New Jedi Order'']]
[[Dark Horse Comics]], which had been contracted to publish ''Star Wars'' [[Star Wars comic books|comics]] since [[1991]],<ref name="Dark Empire 1: The Destiny of a Jedi">[[Dark Empire 1|''Dark Empire'' 1]]</ref> conceived of the idea of an [[Dark Horse invasion storyline|"Invasion" storyline]] rocking the galaxy, and planted the seeds of the eventual story by introducing the character of [[Nom Anor]],<ref name="family dog">{{DHBoards|url=viewtopic.php?t=1962|thread=So Randy Killed the Family Dog?}}</ref> who appeared as an advance agent of what would eventually become the invading forces in [[Crimson Empire II: Council of Blood 1|the first issue]] of [[Star Wars: Crimson Empire II: Council of Blood|''Crimson Empire II: Council of Blood'']], released in [[November]] [[1998]].<ref name="Crimson Empire II: Council of Blood 1">[[Crimson Empire II: Council of Blood 1|''Crimson Empire II: Council of Blood'' 1]]</ref> Following the conception of Anor's character, planning meetings were held at [[Skywalker Ranch]] to discuss what would eventually become ''The New Jedi Order''. Attendants at the meetings included [[Lucas Licensing]] Executive Editor [[Sue Rostoni]], Del Rey Editor at Large [[Shelly Shapiro]], Lucasfilm Director of Publishing [[Lucy Autrey Wilson]],<ref name="Novel Approach" /> Dark Horse Vice President of Publishing [[Randy Stradley]],<ref name="family dog" /> and several of the authors who were already on board,<ref name="Shapiro interview">{{SWArchive|url=eu/lit/novel/f20001224/index.html|text=Telling War Stories: An Interview with Shelly Shapiro}}</ref> including [[Michael A. Stackpole]],<ref name="Cellblock Stackpole">{{WebCite|url=http://theforce.net/jedicouncil/interview/stackpole2.shtml|text=Theforce.net Cellblock 1138 - Mike Stackpole|work=[[TheForce.net]]|archivedate=20200201045327}}</ref> [[James Luceno]],<ref name="Soldiering On">{{WebCite|url=https://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/books/news/2004-06-23-star-wars-books_x.htm|text='Star Wars' books are soldiering on|author=Eng, Dinah|date=2004-06-23|work=[[Wikipedia:USA Today|USA Today]]|archivedate=20191205035824}}</ref> and several comic-book authors.<ref name="Fictional Frontiers">{{WebCite|url=http://www.fictionalfrontiers.podcastpeople.com/posts/27756|text=Interview with James Luceno (Unedited)|author=Awan, Sohaib|date=2008-10-27|work=Fictional Frontiers with Sohaib|archivedate=20160415194057|nolive=1}}</ref> Stradley suggested the invasion idea,<ref name="Family dog page 2">{{DHBoards|url=viewtopic.php?t=1962&postdays=0&postorder=asc&start=15&sid=74fa6a80b216a9bfbd0fa37d55cb09ce|thread=So Randy Killed the Family Dog?}}</ref> and it quickly became the premise of the series. The invaders were conceived of as being users of the [[Dark side of the Force/Legends|dark side of the Force]].<ref name="Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview" />

As many of the novels published under Bantam Spectra had dealt with the [[New Republic/Legends|New Republic's]] continuing war against the [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Empire]],<ref name="Heir to the Empire">''[[Heir to the Empire]]''</ref><ref name="Darksaber">[[Darksaber (novel)|''Darksaber'']]</ref><ref name="X-Wing: Rogue Squadron">''[[X-Wing: Rogue Squadron]]''</ref> it was decided to introduce a newer, larger, and meaner threat.<ref name="Salvatore interview">{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/jedicouncil/interview/salvatore1.shtml|text=Cellblock 1138: R.A. Salvatore - Part 1|work=[[TheForce.net]]|archivedate=20190730151759}}</ref> Shapiro had often heard fans complain that nothing significant happened to any primary character in the novels published by Bantam, and that the galaxy had grown too safe for the heroes, preventing any serious drama from occurring. In the NJO's initial planning sessions, ideas were brainstormed concerning what kind of crisis the characters would face, and everyone present agreed that it was necessary to shake things up by [[Death/Legends|killing]] a previously "invincible" character.<ref name="Shapiro interview" /> The death of [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] was proposed, but the idea was vetoed by [[George Lucas]];<ref name="Novel Approach" /> Lucas also indicated that [[C-3PO/Legends|C-3PO]] and [[R2-D2/Legends|R2-D2]] were off-limits. It was felt that killing [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]], [[Princess/Legends|Princess]] [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia]], or [[Lando Calrissian/Legends|Lando Calrissian]] would not have the dramatic impact that the planning team was looking for, which prompted Stradley to suggest [[Chewbacca/Legends|Chewbacca]], who, despite being a fan-favorite character, had rarely significantly contributed to a novel's plot; Stradley referred to him as the "quintessential supporting character."<ref name="family dog" /> It was felt that the death of a previously untouchable character would give the series its needed emotional impact,<ref name="Salvatore interview page 2" /> but putting Chewbacca on the chopping block was not an easy decision on the part of the creative team, causing many tears to be shed.<ref name="Salvatore interview page 4">{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/jedicouncil/interview/salvatore4.shtml|text=Cellblock 1138: R.A. Salvatore - Part 4|work=[[TheForce.net]]|archivedate=20190730100028}}</ref> Once the final decision was made, Chewbacca's death was approved by George Lucas.<ref name="Salvatore interview page 2" />

[[File:Stradley.jpg|thumb|right|Randy Stradley initially conceived of an "Invasion" storyline, and later suggested that Chewbacca be killed.]]It was also decided during the initial planning stages that a new generation of characters would play a large part in the series.<ref name="Shapiro interview" /> The role of the older generation would remain as important, however—Shapiro indicated that one of the stimuli for the series had been that Bantam authors had been unable to develop Han Solo's character.<ref name="Novel Approach" /> As such, the two generations would star and develop in tandem.<ref name="Shapiro interview" /> At the time, the twin offspring of Han and Leia, [[Darth Caedus|Jacen]] and [[Jaina Solo Fel|Jaina Solo]], were being developed as [[Jedi/Legends|Jedi]]-in-[[Jedi training/Legends|training]] within the pages of the [[Star Wars: Young Jedi Knights|''Young Jedi Knights'']] young reader books,<ref name="Young Jedi Knights">''[[Star Wars: Young Jedi Knights]]''</ref> and the youngest [[Solo family/Legends|Solo]] child, [[Anakin Solo|Anakin]], had recently starred in his own young reader books, the [[Star Wars: Junior Jedi Knights|''Junior Jedi Knights'']] series.<ref name="Junior Jedi Knights">''[[Star Wars: Junior Jedi Knights]]''</ref>

In the early conception of the [[Dark Horse invasion storyline|Bantam/Dark Horse invasion storyline]], initially developed in 1997 and 1998, Anakin was slated to be a prophesied hero, who would be tempted to the dark side by a female infiltrator of the extragalactic Sith invaders of the series, and this would result in a love affair between Anakin and this woman. Jacen and Jaina would eventually confront Anakin, and as a result Jacen would be killed.<ref name="The Essential Reader's Companion">''[[The Essential Reader's Companion]]''</ref> Lucas reviewed this story arc and concluded that it was not original enough, writing in May 1998, "Too redundant to movie storylines - books should be more original."<ref>{{Twitter|infinita|status/324180574031994880|[[Pablo Hidalgo]]|archivedate=20140122191619|quote=and that was listed under the header "Too redundant to movie storylines - books should be more original" (5/6/98) BB memo}}</ref> Lucas also had concerns that in this story pitch, Anakin's story would too closely parallel the story of his grandfather, [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Anakin Skywalker]],<ref>{{Twitter|infinita|status/324181311164137472|[[Pablo Hidalgo]]|quote=circled specifically in the outline is a mention that Anakin Solo "that he is the prophesied one"|archivedate=20140122184439}}</ref><ref name="Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview" /> which was to be told in the forthcoming ''Star Wars'' [[prequel trilogy]].<ref name="Star Wars Episode I: The Phantom Menace">{{Film|I}}</ref> As a result, Lucas advised that Jacen be the primary protagonist of the series instead of Anakin,<ref>{{Twitter|infinita|status/324180106471931904|[[Pablo Hidalgo]]|quote=the note says "Change person who is responsible for ultimately saving the day to Jacen - not Anakin"|archivedate=20140122182926}}</ref> although the story team decided independently that Anakin should die in the series at the midway point, which was approved by Lucas in the final outline. [[Troy Denning]] later corroborated this in a radio interview, remarking that before ''Vector Prime'' was published, it was already written in the story bible of the series that Anakin was slated to die in ''Star by Star'', confirming that this was the intention of the story team before publication of the series began.<ref name="S&V Podcast">{{WebCite|url=http://www.scumandvillainyradio.com/category/directory|text=Directory – "The Priestesses" (Episode #19 for 4/11/2014)|date=2014-04-11|work=Scum and Villainy Radio|archivedate=20140513074211|nolive=1}}</ref>

Lucas also dictated that the villains could not be Force users, stemming from his belief that the Sith could not form a stable "dark side society,"<ref name="Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview" /> and with the encouragement of Lucas to be more original, the authors set to work at constructing a new set of villains with a different story arc for the series. The planning team then constructed a rough outline of the plot direction of the NJO, and chose more authors, some of whom were suggested by Shapiro, and some by Lucas Licensing and subsequently approved by her. The planning team had a general idea of how the series would end, but intended the NJO to be an organic and adaptive series, open to new directions and not carved in stone.<ref name="Shapiro interview" /> The story team consciously made use of mythological archetypes and the hero cycle as delineated by [[Wikipedia:Joseph Campbell|Joseph Campbell]], among others, in their construction of the series.<ref name="Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview" /> James Luceno wrote a "series bible" that would coordinate all of the story arcs and function as a database for new characters and continuity.<ref name="Soldiering On" /><ref name="Wrapping Up">{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/books/unjoh/interviews/lucenotos.html|text=Wrapping It All Up - An Interview with James Luceno|work=[[TheForce.net|The Unofficial NJO Homepage]]|archivedate=20200201043532}}</ref> A continuity team was formed<ref name="tyers9">{{JCFCite|url=posts/1238079|board=Literature|thread=Discuss BP with Kathy -- Spoilers Welcome|user=[[Kathy Tyers|Shmi52]]|date=2000-11-02|time=8:56 AM|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200912114553/https://boards.theforce.net/threads/discuss-bp-with-kathy-spoilers-welcome.1224749/page-10}}</ref> that included Luceno<ref name="Wrapping Up" /> and [[Daniel Wallace]],<ref name="tyers23">{{JCFCite|url=posts/1316251|board=Literature|thread=Discuss BP with Kathy -- Spoilers Welcome|user=[[Kathy Tyers|Shmi53]]|date=2000-11-16|time=8:14 AM|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200312121229/https://boards.theforce.net/threads/discuss-bp-with-kathy-spoilers-welcome.1224749/page-28}}</ref> who expanded the galaxy map<ref name="Wrapping Up" /> and kept track of new Jedi introduced in the series. They encouraged authors to re-use Jedi in later books rather than inventing new ones.<ref name="tyers9" /> Throughout the series' run, Lucasfilm supervised the NJO's production much more closely than they had the Bantam-issued novels.<ref name="tyers6">{{JCFCite|url=posts/1231528|board=Literature|thread=Discuss BP with Kathy -- Spoilers Welcome|user=[[Kathy Tyers|Shmi52]]|date=2000-11-01|time=7:06 AM|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140116225534/http://boards.theforce.net/threads/discuss-bp-with-kathy-spoilers-welcome.1224749/page-7#post-1231528}}</ref>

The series' villains were eventually developed into the [[Yuuzhan Vong]], an [[Extragalactic/Legends|extra-galactic]] race of religious zealots bent on conquering the galaxy. The name "Yuuzhan Vong" was derived from a French-Thai restaurant in New York City where several members of the planning team were eating.<ref name="Novel Approach" /> [[R. A. Salvatore]], Del Rey editor [[Steve Saffel]], and James Luceno became inspired by Aztec, Mayan, and various Mesoamerican ideas for the creation of the mythology and beliefs of the Yuuzhan Vong.<ref name="Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview" /> The final outline of the series was reviewed and approved by Lucas, at which point he ceased his involvement with the series. A [[Pellaeon–Gavrisom Treaty|peace treaty]] with the [[Imperial Remnant|remnants of the Empire]], and the seeds of an alien invasion plotline, were laid in 1998's ''[[Vision of the Future]]'', a novel by [[Timothy Zahn]] published under Bantam's contract.<ref name="Vision of the Future">''[[Vision of the Future]]''</ref>

==Plot summary==
Twenty-one years after the [[Alliance to Restore the Republic/Legends|Rebel Alliance]] destroyed the [[Death Star II/Legends|second]] [[Death Star/Legends|Death Star]], the New Republic is facing internal conflict while trying to maintain peace. A new, unknown enemy called the Yuuzhan Vong emerges from the outer galaxy, beginning what will be known as the [[Yuuzhan Vong War]]. The [[Jedi/Legends|Jedi]], along with the New Republic, struggle to resist this new alien race while it steadily pushes forward, annihilating or occupying different parts of the galaxy.

As the longest continuous series of novels in the Expanded Universe, the NJO established several long-term character arcs. Many new or previously underused characters were put into the spotlight and were developed extensively over the course of the series. Among the most prominent character arcs:

*'''[[Darth Caedus|Jacen Solo]]''': Jacen underwent perhaps the most complete and philosophical arc of the NJO. He began the series as someone who actively questioned whether it was right to use the Force as a weapon. After being captured by the Yuuzhan Vong he withstood weeks of [[Torture/Legends|torture]] at the hands of the Yuuzhan Vong, where upon he is visited and consulted by Vergere, an Old Republic Jedi and Vong familiar. He emerged with a new view of the Force, including a willingness to use it offensively. During the battle to retake [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]], Jacen achieved a state of oneness with the Force that gave him a 'perfect mastery' and also aged him five years. At the end of the series, he was one of the most powerful Jedi in the galaxy.

*'''[[Ganner Rhysode]]''': Rhysode began the series as an arrogant young Jedi, a trait best shown when, on assignment with [[Corran Horn/Legends|Corran Horn]], he mocked Horn's inability to use [[Telekinesis/Legends|telekinesis]], claiming that this made him a lesser Jedi. Rhysode gradually became more humble over the course of the series, especially after watching many of his friends die on the mission to [[Myrkr/Legends|Myrkr]]. After Jacen Solo was captured during this mission, Rhysode went searching for him, even though, in Jacen's words, "we weren't even friends"; Rhysode died fighting thousands of Yuuzhan Vong warriors so that Jacen and [[Vergere]] could escape. It was foreseen that in the future, the Yuuzhan Vong would worship a new god called "the Ganner," referring almost admiringly to Rhysode's last stand.

*'''[[Tahiri Veila]]''': Not fully developed in her original role—Anakin's friend from ''[[Star Wars: Junior Jedi Knights]]''—Tahiri was pushed to a starring role in the NJO. When the Yuuzhan Vong captured [[Yavin 4/Legends|Yavin 4]], she was taken prisoner and subjected to experiments designed to create a Vong-Human hybrid. Anakin eventually rescued her, which stirred the romantic feelings they had had for years; when Anakin died above Myrkr, Tahiri was devastated. At this time, the Yuuzhan Vong personality implanted in her began to periodically take control, and Tahiri would wrestle with this for the duration of the series. Unlike most of the other Jedi, she had a peculiar empathy with the Yuuzhan Vong; at the end of the series, she chose to stay on [[Zonama Sekot/Legends|Zonama Sekot]] in order to continue learning about the Yuuzhan Vong and to help them build a better society.

===The New Order vs. the Old Order===
The Order itself is philosophically distinct from the Jedi Order of the [[Galactic Republic/Legends|Old Republic]] (thus the Old Jedi Order) by a new [[Jedi Code/Legends|Jedi Code]] and broadened view of the Force. The Order's titular head is Luke Skywalker, but other senior Jedi such as [[Kyle Katarn]], [[Mara Jade Skywalker|Mara Jade]] and [[Kyp Durron]] have significant say in the Order's activities. The Order during the Yuuzhan Vong incursion polarizes into two major factions: that of Luke Skywalker, who feels that the Force should be used peacefully, like the Old Republic–era Jedi; and the faction of Kyp Durron, who holds that the Jedi should take a proactive, aggressive stance against enemies, hoping that in that way to live up to the Jedi's roles as "protectors" of the galaxy. As the war proceeded, the aforementioned views of those mentioned evolved, with the most significant evolution in the Jedi thinking being driven by the new philosophical perspectives and development of Jacen Solo and Vergere.

===A New Era and New Alliances===
At the beginning of the series, the galaxy is enjoying a period of newly established peacetime between the New Republic and the Imperial Remnant. As the galaxy is increasingly antagonized by the invading Yuuzhan Vong forces, this peacetime neutrality soon gives way to new alliances, as the Imperial Remnant and the New Republic periodically work together throughout the series to ward off the extragalactic invaders. Other factions, like the [[Hapes Consortium/Legends|Hapes Consortium]], the [[Hutt Space/Legends|Hutts]], the [[Chiss/Legends|Chiss]], and the [[Peace Brigade]], also play important roles through the series in the shifting of alliances. By the end of the war, the combined forces of the galaxy unite to form the [[Galactic Federation of Free Alliances]].

===Character deaths===
A number of important characters die within the ''New Jedi Order'' series, as the galaxy is challenged by a brutal and mysterious enemy, the likes of which had not been encountered during the earlier years of the New Republic. The most significant deaths for fans were Chewbacca and Anakin Solo, both of which had dramatic emotional impacts on the principal characters of the series. In a number of ''New Jedi Order'' books, other Jedi also are lost in the course of the war against the Yuuzhan Vong, prompting Jedi Master Luke Skywalker to create a temporary praxeum to protect Jedi children at a [[Shelter|hidden space station]] in [[Maw Cluster/Legends|the Maw]]. Some planets, like [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]] and [[Ithor/Legends|Ithor]], were completely ravaged by the Yuuzhan Vong as a result of their [[Terraforming/Legends|terraforming]] upon conquest. In total, a large number of the galactic population perished as a result of the bloody war, with the combined forces of the galaxy eventually winning the war and triumphantly ending the conflict under the newly established [[Galactic Federation of Free Alliances|Galactic Alliance]] government.

While [[Mon Mothma/Legends|Mon Mothma]] did not die in the ''New Jedi Order'' series, she died directly prior to ''Vector Prime''; her death was first revealed in the series.

==Continuity==
===Galactic maps===
The ''New Jedi Order'' series was the first ''Star Wars'' product to include fully drawn maps of the galaxy at the start of the novels; five maps were created in total, showing the different stages of the war and the invasion path of the Yuuzhan Vong. The maps were illustrated by artist [[Chris Barbieri|Christopher Barbieri]], and the layout of the galaxy was constructed throughout 1998 and 1999 by James Luceno and Daniel Wallace. Every novel included a map of the galaxy, at that particular period in the war.<ref name="Making the Map">{{InsiderCite|65|Making the Map}}</ref><gallery>
File:NJOmaps_VP.png|''Vector Prime'' (25 ABY)
File:NJOmaps_BP.png|''Balance Point'' (26 ABY)
File:NJOmaps_ELI.png|''Enemy Lines I'' (27 ABY)
File:NJOmaps_DW.png|''Destiny's Way'' (28 ABY)
File:NJOmaps_TUF.png|''The Unifying Force'' (29 ABY)
</gallery>

==Release and reception==
[[File:VectorPrime_Promo_1999.png|thumb|''Vector Prime'' promo]]
The publication of ''The New Jedi Order'' was anticipated by magazine ads, posters, postcards, a [[Vector Prime television commercial|television commercial]], and later author tours to promote the series. When ''Vector Prime'' initially released, it spent four weeks on the ''New York Times'' Bestsellers List.<ref name="bp" /> Following this initial success, every subsequent novel in the series—all 19 books—would make the Bestsellers List.<ref>{{Reddit|url=r/StarWarsEU/comments/muse38/flashback_every_single_novel_of_the_19book_new|thread=Flashback: Every single novel of the 19-book 'New Jedi Order' series became a New York Times Bestseller on release|archivedate=20250322174522}}</ref> This feat would go on to make it the longest continuous NYT Bestselling series in ''Star Wars'' history. By the end of 2005, the series had sold over 4 million books worldwide.<ref>{{RandomHouseOld|url=author/results.pperl?authorid=82866|text=Matthew Stover}}|url=Star-Wars-Unseen-Queen-Dark/dp/0099491060|text=''Star Wars: The Unseen Queen (Dark Nest II)''}}. Amazon.co.uk. "The second book in a trilogy that will bridge the events of the hugely popular New Jedi Order series, which has sold four million copies to date." Published October 2005.</ref> The death of Chewbacca in ''Vector Prime'' received widespread coverage in the form of articles, memorials, and tributes by newspapers across the United States after the book's release.<ref>{{JCFCite|date=2021-05-22|time=2:44 PM|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20210326232446/https://boards.theforce.net/threads/flashback-national-newspaper-coverage-of-the-finale-of-vector-prime-chewbacca.50055882|board=Literature|thread=Flashback: National Newspaper Coverage of the Finale of "Vector Prime"/Chewbacca|url=threads/50055882|user=xezene}}</ref> The final book of the series, ''The Unifying Force,'' spent two weeks on the Bestsellers List and was promoted by a six-city author tour for the novel, with James Luceno visiting Seattle, Indianapolis, Phoenix, Minneapolis, San Diego and Washington, D.C to promote the novel, as well as giving radio interviews and giving away ''New Jedi Order'' baseball caps at the events.<ref>[https://www.publishersweekly.com/pw/print/20031117/34011-jedi-s-closing-hit.html Jedi's Closing Hit]. ''Publisher's Weekly''. November 2003.</ref> The publication of the series also spawned the release of a number of [[Hasbro]] action figures for the series, as part of its [[Star Wars: The Legacy Collection|Legacy]] and [[Star Wars: The Vintage Collection|Vintage Collections]].

R. A. Salvatore remarked in 2015 at the Salt Lake Comic Con that most of the response to ''Vector Prime'' that he had received was positive, despite a small number of fans sending death threats over the death of Chewbacca.<ref name=":1">[https://bigshinyrobot.com/books/star-wars-why-we-killed-chewie Star Wars: Why We Killed Chewie]. ''Big Shiny Robot.'' October 2, 2015.</ref> Michael A. Stackpole also commented positively upon the series at the event, stating that "It made the characters vulnerable. It made people actually worry when it looked like someone was going to die."<ref name=":1" /> Troy Denning remarked upon working on the series with such an ensemble cast of writers, saying, "It was a lot of fun. The brainstorming sessions we had at Skywalker Ranch were just a blast," while noting the high level of coordination it required between so many different authors sometimes posed "a little bit more of a coordination headache," requiring authors to coordinate with authors several books ahead and several books behind their point in the series.<ref name="S&V Podcast" /> Denning would remark, "As many pages as we wrote in manuscript, we probably wrote that many pages again in emails" simply to make the series all fit together from book to book.<ref name="S&V Podcast" /> [[Walter Jon Williams]], author of ''Destiny's Way'', said in 2009, "I got more positive fan reaction from that one book than from anything else I've ever written, and I'd very much like to thank the fans for reading so carefully, and for caring."<ref>{{WordPress|subdomain=roqoodepot|name=Roqoo Depot|url=interviews/walter-jon-williams|text=Walter Jon Williams|date=2009-01-16|archivedate=20211020072131}}</ref> [[James Luceno]], principal architect of the ''New Jedi Order'' and the author who reviewed every manuscript of the series as it came in, remarked after the completion of the series, "From the start, The New Jedi Order drew a sizable readership, even in the face of competition from the prequel novels, the comics, and other Star Wars related series. So there's some sense of accomplishment in having been able to hold the interest of so many readers. It was also gratifying to see Troy Denning, Greg Keyes, Matt Stover, and others receive much-deserved acclaim for their individual efforts."<ref name="Wrapping Up" />

Timothy Zahn felt the deaths of major characters in ''The New Jedi Order'' and subsequent ''Star Wars'' book series strayed from the "feel" of ''Star Wars'', stating that though second-tier characters might die in war, he believed first-tier characters should always survive each story.<ref name="Zahn">{{WebCite|url=http://fractalmatter.com/main/?p=763|text=Timothy Zahn and Star Wars|author=Ross|work=Fractal Matter|quote=I don't think that killing off major characters really fits the style and overall "feel" of the Star Wars universe. My take on Star Wars is that it's an old-fashioned good-versus-evil saga where the heroes fight against impossible odds and eventually triumph. In the process, second-and third-tier characters might die (people DO die in war, after all), but the first-tier characters make it through alive. […] I disagreed with Chewbacca's death, too, for the same reasons.|archivedate=20080112051504|nolive=1}}</ref> He did mention, however, "I'm not really familiar with the overall story line of that series, so I can't really comment on whether the death was a necessary part of the story line."<ref name="Zahn" /> The ''New Jedi Order'' authors and editors felt that after almost a decade of Bantam stories, there was a demand for stories that could risk the primary characters' lives in real ways, where not everyone was guaranteed to make it through to the end. On this subject, during the [[Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview|Round Robin New Jedi Order Interview]], editor Lucy Wilson reflected, "Good things happen and bad things happen in the Star Wars universe—as in our own world."<ref name="Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview" /> Sue Rostoni added: "We wanted the NJO stories to have more of the feel of reality, with conflict and emotion. By shaking up the universe, we felt we were adding an emotional depth to the stories that wasn't there before, and we were confident that our readers were up to the challenge."<ref name="Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview" />

In an interview published in ''[[The New Jedi Order: The Unifying Force]]'', Shelly Shapiro, the Editorial Director of Del Rey, expressed some regret over the timing of the novel ''[[The New Jedi Order: Star by Star]]'', coming so soon after the [[Wikipedia:September 11, 2001 attacks|September 11 attacks]].<ref name="Star Wars: New Jedi Order Round-Robin Interview" />

==Adaptations and tie-in material==
[[File:VP_ad.png|thumb|[[Vector Prime television commercial|''Vector Prime'' commercial]]]]
In 1999, [[Mark Hamill]] reprised his role as Luke Skywalker in a brief speaking role for the series. Hamill spoke as Luke Skywalker in a [[Vector Prime television commercial|television commercial]] for the first novel in the series, ''Vector Prime''. In the commercial, released on the [[Wikipedia:Sci Fi Channel (United States)|Sci Fi Channel]] in late [[1999]], he recalls the state that the galaxy is in at the present, compared to the times of the Empire.<ref>{{YouTube|video=bOV2dGdFbGk|text=Vector Prime Commercial|channel=channel/UCQ5GqobaluC-IHL-LOS04Yg|name=darthleland66}}</ref> An independently remastered version of the commercial was later released on YouTube.<ref>{{YouTube|video=WVCV552EPGk|text=Remastered Vector Prime Commercial|channel=@holonetwork|name=holonet}}</ref>
